flashcards.Back: The original security protocol for 802.11 Wi-Fi. it uses the RC4 cipher and is now considered completely insecure and deprecated.
A legacy security standard designed to fix WEP's flaws. it introduced TKIP for encryption and dynamic key management.
The industry standard for wireless security that fully implements 802.11i. it uses robust AES encryption with the CCMP algorithm.
The latest wireless security standard that adds mandatory PMF and replaces PSK with SAE to prevent offline dictionary attacks.
The powerful encryption algorithm used in WPA2 and WPA3. it is much more secure than the older RC4 cipher used in WEP.
Temporal Key Integrity Protocol. A stopgap encryption method for WPA that used longer initialization vectors but has since been deprecated.
Counter Mode with Cipher Block Chaining Message Authentication Code Protocol. The specific algorithm used by WPA2 for data privacy and integrity.
Galois/Counter Mode Protocol. The high-performance encryption and integrity algorithm used in WPA3 to provide even stronger protection than CCMP.
Also known as Personal Mode. A security method where all users on a wireless network use the same static password to authenticate.
The key exchange protocol in WPA3-Personal that protects users from brute-force password guessing attacks.
Also known as Enterprise Mode. A port-based framework that requires unique user credentials verified against a RADIUS server.
A flexible framework used by 802.1X to carry different authentication methods between the client and the AAA server.
A high-security EAP method that requires digital certificates on both the client and the server for mutual authentication.
Protected EAP. A method that creates a secure TLS tunnel to protect the exchange of user credentials, typically using MS-CHAPv2.
The role played by the wireless client device (e.g., a laptop or smartphone) in an 802.1X authentication process.
The role played by the access point or Wireless LAN Controller that acts as an intermediary between the client and the AAA server.
A security feature that encrypts 802.11 management frames, such as deauthentication packets, to prevent disconnection attacks.
A hashing function used in WPA/WPA2/WPA3 to ensure that wireless frames have not been tampered with in transit.
A random number used along with a secret key for data encryption. WEP's IV was too short (24 bits), making it easy to crack.
A property of WPA3 that ensures even if a future key is compromised, it cannot be used to decrypt past wireless traffic.
The process used by WPA2 and WPA3 to securely derive and install unique encryption keys for a client session.
The structure of different keys (PMK, PTK, GTK) used in Wi-Fi security to protect both unicast and group traffic.
A shared encryption key used by an access point to protect broadcast and multicast traffic sent to all associated clients.
A unique encryption key used to protect unicast traffic between a specific wireless client and the access point.
Flexible Authentication via Secure Tunneling. A Cisco-proprietary EAP method that uses Protected Access Credentials (PAC) for secure login.
The centralized server (typically RADIUS) that makes the final 'accept' or 'reject' decision in an 802.1X wireless environment.
A security process where both the client and the network prove their identities to each other before data is exchanged.
The legacy stream cipher used by WEP. its weaknesses led to the development of the more robust AES-based security standards.
The specific MIC algorithm used in the TKIP standard to provide data integrity for WPA networks.
A configuration where no password is required to join the wireless network. data is transmitted without encryption and is visible to anyone nearby.
